name: cajun chicken fricassee
instructions:
POACH THE CHICKEN: cook the chicken in bouillon until done. Set the chicken aside; reserve the broth.

SAUTE THE HOLY TRINITY: in a separate pan, sauté white onions, bell peppers, and celery in vegetable oil and ghee. Add garlic and jalapeños once the trinity is softened.

BUILD THE SAUCE: sprinkle in all-purpose flour and cook for a minute to lose the raw flour flavor. Add cajun blend and thyme. Deglaze with the reserved bouillon. Simmer to thicken.

FINISH: stir the cooked chicken back into the sauce. A few dashes of hot sauce (cholula or similar) at this stage adds depth. Squeeze lime over the top before serving.
notes:
Cajun analogue of the french chicken fricassee. Same structural recipe (chicken + fat-and-flour-and-aromatics + broth), different cuisine register.

DICING THE TRINITY (the most important technique point): the onions, bell peppers, and celery need to be small but not pulverized. Knife-and-cutting-board is too coarse — you get chunks that don't melt into the sauce. A blender is too fine — you get a green-grey goo that's structurally wrong for fricassee. A grater or food processor is the ideal middle ground: small enough that the trinity dissolves into the sauce body, large enough that the pieces still hold their shape. This is what gives the dish its cajun-fricassee texture rather than a soup-with-chunks or a baby-food paste.

WHAT MAKES IT CAJUN INSTEAD OF FRENCH:
- HOLY TRINITY: white onions + bell peppers + celery in place of the french leek-bell pepper-mushroom base. This is the foundational cajun aromatic base, equivalent to the french soffrito or the spanish sofrito.
- CAJUN BLEND: doing the structural seasoning work that thyme alone does in the french version. Thyme still rides alongside — it's one of the cajun blend's natural partners — but the cajun blend is the main flavor.
- LIME + JALAPENOS: the acid + heat axis is cajun-shaped rather than french (which would be white wine + tarragon).
- VEGETABLE OIL + GHEE (NOT BUTTER): the cajun version leans on neutral oil + ghee, not butter. Butter + garlic dangerously swings the dish back toward a generic french register, which is exactly what the recipe is trying to escape. Veggie oil keeps the fat profile neutral and lets the cajun blend lead; ghee adds depth that helps the spices bloom. Butter is left in as a secondary option, but the default is no butter.
- HOT SAUCE (cholula or similar): a few dashes during the finish does the same kind of work worcestershire does in european beef stews — adds vinegar-acid depth and umami that helps round out the dish. Recommended.

NO CREAM: the french version finishes in heavy cream. The cajun version doesn't — the roux carries the body, and the cajun spice profile would fight cream.

GREEN ONIONS / SCALLIONS: common cajun garnish. Thinly sliced and scattered over the top at serving time. Doesn't change the structural recipe but the bright sharp finish is canonical for cajun stews and gumbos.

MAY 17 2026 EXPERIMENT: tried cheese grater + microplane instead of cutting board for the trinity dicing. Findings:
- CHEESE GRATER: good for the trinity. Produces the small-but-structural texture that knife-and-board can't.
- MICROPLANE: not good for onions or bell peppers. Just made a mess and the juices got everywhere. Microplane is for hard things (garlic, ginger, citrus zest) — not high-water-content vegetables.
- CELERY: didn't do enough this time. Squeezed the juices out of the grated mixture at the end thinking it was too wet, but most of the flavor left with the liquid. Lesson: don't drain the trinity after grating — that's the flavor. Just sauté longer to drive off water if needed.
- CHOLULA HOT SAUCE: tried adding cholula during the finish, helped a lot. Worth keeping as a default addition (see HOT SAUCE bullet above).
- VEGGIE OIL OVER BUTTER: skipped butter in favor of veggie oil this time. Result was noticeably more cajun-leaning — butter + garlic was pushing the dish toward a generic french register without my realizing it. Recipe updated to reflect this.

Overall: better attempt than the first one. Next iteration: more celery, no juice-squeezing, keep the veggie-oil-and-ghee fat profile.
